segments.s693-g81: بیامد is the past tense verb 'came' (third-person singular). ز نزدیک is a poetic word order for از نزدیک meaning 'from/coming up close' (ز = 'from', نزدیک = 'near'). خسرو سوار is a noun phrase where خسرو qualifies سوار: 'a rider of Khosrow' (possessor/name before the noun). The whole segment thus means 'a rider of Khosrow came up close.'
segments.s693-g82: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' linking the action to what follows. تا here has the sense 'as far as/up to' (not temporal 'until'); در is used as the noun 'door/gate' (not the preposition 'in'), so در قیصر = 'the gate/portal of the Caesar'. نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned' modifying قیصر. Together: 'thus up to the renowned Caesar's gate.'
segments.s693-g1: This clause opens with the preposition ز meaning 'because of/from' followed by بیگانه 'the stranger' as the cause. قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar'. به + پرداخت جای is a purposive/locative phrase: به here introduces the action/direction and پرداخت (an older/poetic verbal noun) denotes the act of attending to or handling something, while جای 'place' names the location (or figurative 'place' of taking up the matter). Word order is causal phrase → subject → prepositional phrase, typical of Persian word order in narrative.
segments.s693-g2: پر اندیشه is an adjectival phrase 'full of thought' (پر = 'full (of)', modifying اندیشه 'thought'). بنشست is the past tense singular 'he sat down' (archaic/poetic past form of نشستن). با رهنمای is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the adviser' (با + noun). The whole segment describes Caesar's manner/state as he sits with his adviser.
segments.s693-g3: به موبد is a dative/prepositional phrase 'to the mobad' (موبد = Zoroastrian priest). چنین گفت literally 'said thus' introduces direct speech that follows. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee, and دادخواه is the vocative noun 'seeker of justice' (an honorific or form of address). The segment sets up an utterance directed to the priest.
segments.s693-g4: This is the content of the speech. ز گیتی is an ablative/agentive phrase 'from the world' or 'the world has ...'; گرفتست is an archaic perfect/passive-like form meaning 'has taken/has seized' (here transitive). ما را is the object 'us' marked by را, and پناه is the object noun 'refuge/protection'. Together the clause says that the world has taken away/denied them refuge. Word order follows classical Persian narrative: oblique phrase → verb → object.
segments.s693-g5: بسازیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us make/prepare' (a proposal to act). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/that', followed by او 'he' as the beneficiary/target, بنیرو is an ablative/instrumental phrase meaning 'with force/by strength' (بـ + نیرو contracted), and شود is the subjunctive/potential 'may become' or simple present/future 'becomes'. The clause expresses intention: we will make him so that he becomes powerful.
segments.s693-g6: وزان is a conjunctive/locative 'and from that / thereby'. کهتر 'the lesser' (an adjective used as substantive) and بد 'bad/ill' describe the opposing party; they form a nominal cluster describing someone diminished or unfortunate. بی‌آهوشود is a fused verbal form 'becomes without a gazelle' (literally 'becomes gazelle-less'), an idiomatic/poetic expression here meaning 'becomes bereft/deprived of his prize'. The clause states the expected consequence for the weaker party. (This segment contains a poetic idiom; exact nuance is contextual.)
segments.s693-g7: به قیصر 'to the Caesar' (dative), چنین گفت 'said thus', پس is an adverb 'then/thereupon', and رهنمای is the agent 'the adviser' (here performing the speech). The order places the addressee before the report of speech; 'پس رهنمای' signals that the adviser now speaks after the previous speaker/action.
segments.s693-g8: که introduces a content clause 'that/which' (here beginning what the adviser will say). از فیلسوفان 'from among the philosophers' is a partitive/genitive phrase, پاکیزه رای combines an adjective 'پاکیزه' ('pure/clean') with رای 'opinion/counsel' to mean 'of pure counsel' or 'of sound counsel'. This segment functions as the topic/qualification of the adviser’s forthcoming statement (the rest of the sentence would complete the thought).
segments.s693-g57: نبرداشتند is a negative past/pluperfect-form verb meaning 'they did not accept/tolerate' (here with transitive sense). Word order is verb-first (typical poetic inversion): نبرداشتند (predicate) + از کسی (genitive/agent 'from anyone' = 'anyone's') + سرکشی (object 'arrogance/rebellion').
segments.s693-g58: A simple asyndetic nominal series describing qualities they did not tolerate. بلندی, تندی, بی‌دانشی are three nouns joined by و; they enumerate 'height/arrogance', 'fierceness/violence', and 'ignorance'.
segments.s693-g59: کنون is a fronted adverb 'now'; این سخنها is the topical noun phrase 'these words'. نیارد بها is the verbal predicate 'do not gain/receive value' (بها = 'value/price'); نیارد is an archaic/poetic negative verb form here meaning 'does not obtain/bring'.
segments.s693-g60: که introduces a clause of explanation ('for/that'), باشد is a present/subjunctive form of 'to be' (introducing the predication), سراندر is a prepositional compound 'in/within', دم here means 'breath' or 'moment', and اژدها is the noun 'dragon' — together: 'that it is within the dragon's breath' = 'that it counts for nothing when the dragon's breath is upon one.'
segments.s693-g61: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'; سوی قیصر is the directional phrase 'to/unto Caesar'; بر here functions in the colloquial/poetic sense of movement toward (go/unto); از من درود is the object 'a greeting from me' (از marks source). The whole unit means 'send someone to Caesar with my greeting.'
segments.s693-g62: بگویش is an imperative/optative 'tell him' (بگو + ش = 'to him'); که introduces reported content ('that'); گفتار is the noun 'speech/utterance'; بی‌تار و پود is a fixed metaphorical phrase 'without warp and weft' (literally 'without warp and weft' — i.e., lacking structure or substance). Grammatically this is a reported clause: 'tell him that the speech is without warp and weft.'
segments.s693-g63: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones / great men'; نیارند is the predicate here (an archaic/poetic verb form whose exact lemma in the supplied senses doesn't match — functionally 'do not bring/ present/ place'), and پیش خرد is a prepositional phrase 'before wisdom' (پیش = 'in front of/before', خرد = 'wisdom'). Together: 'Great men do not bring themselves before wisdom' (i.e. they defer to wisdom).
segments.s693-g64: به فرجام is a circumstantial phrase 'to/at the end' (فرجام = 'end/outcome'); هم is an adverb 'also/even'; نیک و بد are the contrasted nouns 'good and bad'; بگذرد is a present/subjunctive or general present 'passes/comes to pass' — together: 'in the end even good and bad pass.'
segments.s693-g25: هم اندر زمان here gives a temporal emphasis 'even/at that time'. باژ is a noun 'cry' (battle/shout) and خواهد is the 3rd-person singular auxiliary used futurally ('will be'); ز روم is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from Rome' giving the source. Word order is poetic (noun + auxiliary + source) rather than a more prosaic 'از روم باژ خواهد بود'.
segments.s693-g26: بپا اندر آرد is a compact poetic clause: آرد ('brings/causes') governed by the same future sense as خواهد earlier, so the sense is 'will set up/raise'. بپا (here 'set up/bring to standing') + اندر (in/throughout) forms the verbal idea, and همه مرز وبوم is the direct object 'all the borders and the land.' The verb appears after its modifiers in the inverted, elevated poetic order.
segments.s693-g27: گرین درخورد با خرد یاد دار is an imperative/hortative instruction: گرین 'tearful/mournful' qualifies the kind of 'یاد' (remembrance); درخورد ('worthy, fitting') describes that remembrance; با خرد 'with wisdom' is an instrumental phrase, and یاد دار (literally 'hold/keep in memory') is the verbal phrase. The order is adjective + qualifying adjective + prepositional phrase + verbal phrase typical of classical Persian.
segments.s693-g28: سخنهای ایرانیان باد دار is a short optative clause: سخنهای ایرانیان 'the words/sayings of the Iranians' is the subject, باد is the classical optative particle meaning 'may (it be)', and دار here functions as a stative/possessive element meaning 'have/keep' or 'endure' (an archaic usage). Together: 'May the words of the Iranians endure.'
segments.s693-g29: ازیشان چوبشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard of/about them': از ایشان 'concerning/of them' and چوبشنید is the contracted poetic fusion of چو + بشنید ('when he heard'). قیصر سخن follows as the topic/agent and what he heard—'the Caesar heard (those) words.'
segments.s693-g30: یکی دیگر اندیشه افگند بن is a simple past clause: یکی دیگر 'another/a certain other (one)' + اندیشه 'thought' is the object, and افگند is the past of 'to cast/throw' used figuratively 'he cast/formed (a) thought' = 'he conceived another thought.' بن at the end is an archaic particle/interjection ('thereupon/then') used for emphasis; its form and function are poetic/archaic.
segments.s693-g31: سواری فرستاد نزدیک شاه is a past-tense clause in which the direct object سواری ('a rider/horseman') comes before the verb فرستاد ('sent'). نزدیک شاه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to (or near) the king' — i.e., 'to the king's presence.' The syntax (object-verb and then location) is normal for classical Persian.
segments.s693-g32: یکی نامه بنوشت و بنمود راه contains two coordinated past actions joined by و. یکی (here 'someone/a certain one') is the subject, نامه 'a letter' the object, and بنوشت is the poetic/past form 'wrote' (written as one word here). بنمود ('showed' or 'pointed out') takes راه 'the way/path' as its object. The sequence means 'someone wrote a letter and showed/indicated the way.'
segments.s693-g17: ز بس ... is a causal phrase: ز here means "because of" (poetic preposition) and بس quantifies it as "so much / an abundance of." The following nouns are a coordinated list (غارت و جنگ و آویختن) giving the causes: غارت (plunder), جنگ (war), and آویختن (hanging/executions). The conjunction و simply links the items in the usual Persian left-to-right order.
segments.s693-g18: همان بی‌گنه خیره خون ریختن is a nominal/participial clause describing the victims and the act: همان 'that/the very', بی‌گنه 'innocent', خیره used adjectivally as 'staring/astonished/dazed', and خون ریختن is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to spill/shedding blood.' The clause lacks an explicit finite verb and functions as a consequence of the preceding causal phrase (i.e. 'and from that — the innocent, stunned, blood was shed').
segments.s693-g19: This is a subject and causal phrase: کنون 'now' (temporal adverb) introduces the line, پاک یزدان is the compound subject 'the pure God', and ز کردار بد is a causal prepositional phrase (ز = 'because of', followed by the noun + adjective 'کردار بد' = 'bad deed/evil conduct'). Word order places the time and subject before the causal phrase.
segments.s693-g20: به پیش اندر آوردشان کار بد is a verbal clause describing what God does: به پیش ("before, to the presence of") + اندر (classical directional particle) combine with آوردشان (آورد + ـشان = 'brings/causes upon them' or 'brings them') and the object/accusative phrase کار بد ('an evil affair' / 'bad outcome'). In classical Persian the order can be somewhat flexible; here the verbal complex carries the meaning 'brought upon them a bad fate' or 'has set their bad deed before (them/him).' آروردشان is the compound verb nucleus with the attached plural object pronoun -شان.
segments.s693-g21: This is an imperative nominal phrase: یکی 'one / any (one)', خامشی 'a silence / hush', and برگزین is the imperative 'choose!' The prepositional phrase از میان 'from among' specifies the domain of choice; note the object-first order (یکی خامشی) followed by the imperative, which is common in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s693-g22: چوشد is a contracted classical form (چو + شود) meaning 'when/if it becomes.' Here it governs a verbless nominal clause where کندرو (frankincense) is the predicate/figurative outcome and بخت ساسانیان ('the fortune of the Sasanians') is the subject; literally, 'when the Sasanians' fortune becomes frankincense.' The line is metaphorical and uses the inverted, terse word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s693-g23: This is the protasis (conditional clause): اگر 'if' introduces the condition, خسرو is the subject (the king Khosrow), آن خسروانی کلاه is the object phrase 'that royal hat/cap' with آن 'that' pointing to a specific item and خسروانی as an adjective 'royal.' Word order is standard for a conditional noun phrase.
segments.s693-g24: بدست آورد is a classical compound meaning 'to obtain' (بدست here is part of the compound 'به‌دست آوردن'). سر بر آرد is a small clause meaning 'he lifts/raises his head' (سر direct object + بر آرد where آرد is the archaic/present-tense form 'he brings/raises'). بماه is a simile/adverbial 'like the moon' or 'to the moon,' describing the manner/state of the raised head. The verb forms are classical/presentive used to express the predicted result.
segments.s693-g9: بباید is an impersonal/modal verb meaning "it is necessary / one must" (here functioning as "we must"); it appears before the noun phrase for emphasis. تنی چند is a quantifying phrase literally "a few bodies" (i.e. "a few people"), with تنی (a certain/some) + چند (several/a few). بیداردل is a descriptive compound (postpositive adjective) meaning "wakeful‑hearted" or "alert‑hearted" modifying the quantified people.
segments.s693-g10: که introduces a relative clause "who/that" describing those people. بندند is the 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive (classical style) of بستـن/بستن meaning "to bind/attach" (here "will bind"); it takes دل (heart) as its object. با ما = "with us." بدین کار is an older phrasing of به + این کار, "to/for this work/task" (ب here is the preposition "to/for" and دین in the manuscript segmentation corresponds to the cliticized demonstrative "این" in classical orthography — functionally "this"); دل ("heart") is the thing being bound to the work.
segments.s693-g11: فرستاد is the simple past 3rd‑person form "sent." Here the verb precedes its arguments in typical epic word order; کس is the indefinite object "someone," and قیصر نامدار is the agent (the famed Caesar), with نامدار as a postpositive adjective "renowned/famed."
segments.s693-g12: برفتند is past plural "they went/they departed." زان is the poetic prepositional particle meaning "from that / of those" (here linking back to the philosophers just mentioned). فیلسوفان is the plural noun "philosophers"; the numeral چهار (placed at the end in the line) specifies how many went ("four"). The clause therefore means "four of those philosophers went forth."
segments.s693-g13: جوانان و پیران is a coordinated noun phrase "young (people) and elders." رومی نژاد is an adjectival phrase meaning "of Roman (Rûm) lineage/stock," where رومی qualifies نژاد ("race/lineage"). Word order is noun(s) + descriptive phrase for origin.
segments.s693-g14: سخنهای دیرینه is a noun phrase "ancient/old sayings/words" (سخنهای = plural "words/sayings", دیرینه = "ancient/long‑standing"). کردند یاد is a light verb construction: کردند (they did/made, past plural of کردن) combines with the noun یاد ("remembrance/recall") to mean "they remembered/recalled" or "they made mention of" the ancient words.
segments.s693-g15: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause "that..." ما is the subject "we." تا + [temporal clause] (تا سکندر بشد زین جهان) uses تا as "until"; سکندر is the noun "Alexander." بشد is classical past/past‑subjunctive of شدن meaning "became/appeared" (here "came/appeared on the world/stage"). زین جهان is an older phrasing for "in/onto this world" (زین is an archaic demonstrative "this" + جهان "world").
segments.s693-g16: ز is the poetic/prepositional "from/of" marking origin. ایرانیانیم is the predicate "we are Iranians / of Iranian stock" (ایرانیان + -یم "we are"). خسته is an adjective "weary/tired," and نهان is an adverb/adjective "hidden/secretly." The sequence places the origin statement and then characterizes the state as "secretly/hiddenly weary."
segments.s693-g73: به ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase marking the indirect object: 'to the Iranians'. گفت is a past-tense verb 'he said' that introduces reported speech. فرمان کنید is an imperative plural: فرمان (noun 'command') + کنید (second-person plural imperative of کردن) = 'give/issue orders' or 'command (you all)'.
segments.s693-g74: دل خویش را is the object phrase: دل 'heart' + خوی + ش forming خویش 'one's own' (possessive), followed by را the definite/direct-object marker. زین سخن is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning 'from/with these words' (زین = 'from/this'), and مشکنید is a negative imperative (plural) from شکستن: 'do not break'. The whole clause is the content of the reported order.
segments.s693-g75: که at the start introduces an explanatory/casual clause here ('for'/'because'). یزدان پیروزگر is a noun phrase with پیروزگر as an adjectival epithet 'victorious' modifying 'God'. یار ماست is a copular clause: یار 'ally' + ما (possessive 'our') + ست (poetic/clipped form of است) = 'is our ally.'
segments.s693-g76: جوانمردی و مردمی forms a coordinated noun phrase: جوانمردی 'chivalry/heroism' and مردمی 'of the people' (modifier). کارماست is a copular expression: کار 'affair/work' + ماست ('is ours' with ست = است) = 'is our concern/affair.'
segments.s693-g77: گرفت is a past-tense verb 'he took' or 'he seized'. این سخن is the direct object 'these/that words'. بردل is a prepositional/compound adverb meaning 'into (his) heart' (بر + دل in older poetic order). خوی + ش again forms خویش 'his/one's own' as a possessive suffix on 'دل'. خوار here describes the person (the despicable one/wretch) related to the action.
segments.s693-g78: فرستاد is a past-tense verb 'he sent'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. بدست in this poetic formula functions as 'into the hand(s) of' or 'into the possession of'. تخوار (here likely a contemptuous epithet 'the wretch' or a lowly person) names the recipient.
segments.s693-g79: برین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner' formed from بر + این (poetic contraction) + گونه 'manner/kind'. برنامه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a plan/proclamation/arrangement' (the -ای ending giving indefiniteness), and برنوشت is the past tense verb 'he wrote/inscribed (upon)' in poetic diction, linking the action to the manner.
segments.s693-g80: ز هرگونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'of/from every kind' (ز as poetic 'of/from' + هرگونه‌ای 'each/any kind'). اندر here reinforces inclusion 'in/among'. و خوب و زشت lists the polar qualities 'good and ugly' (i.e., 'both fair and foul') describing the kinds covered by the preceding phrase.
segments.s693-g49: به بیداد ... جنگ کردند is literal word order where به + noun marks the target/against whom the action is done: 'they made war against oppression.' کردند is the past plural of کردن, here 'to wage/make (war)'. ار begins a conditional 'if' introducing the clause that follows (short for "اگر").
segments.s693-g50: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see'; تا here introduces purpose/result meaning 'so that' or 'in order that' (i.e. 'see, so that...'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پیران is the plural 'elders'; که introduces a relative clause 'who'; دارد is present 'has' (possesses) and بیاد is the phrase 'in memory' — together 'who have it in memory' = 'who remember it.'
segments.s693-g51: سزدگر is a particle/agentive phrase meaning 'one who deems it fitting' or 'if it is proper'; بپرسد is a jussive/present-subjunctive form (third person) used here as 'let him ask' or 'he should ask'; ز marks source 'from' and دانای روم is a genitive phrase 'the wise one of Rome' (i.e. the Roman/Byzantine sage).
segments.s693-g52: که introduces an indirect question 'whether'; این بد = 'this evil/ill'; ز + زاغ = 'from the crow' (zāgh as omen/rook); آمدست is an archaic perfect form 'has come'; ار here means 'or if' introducing the alternative 'زبوم' — 'from the land/earth' (i.e. whether the cause is the bird or the land).
segments.s693-g53: The opening که marks a explanatory/relative clause. هرکس که 'whoever' begins the generalizing clause; در رزم 'in battle' is the locative phrase; شد is past/become and Sرفراز is the predicate meaning 'victorious' — literally 'whoever in battle became victorious.'
segments.s693-g54: همی here is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/so'); ز marks cause/agency 'by' or 'from' the agent that follows; آفریننده 'the Creator' is the agent; شد 'became' again is the copula and بی‌نیاز is the resulting predicate 'self‑sufficient' or 'free from need.'
segments.s693-g55: نیاکان ما is 'our ancestors' (possessive order: ancestors of us); نامداران is the predicate 'renowned/illustrious'; بدند is the archaic past copula 'they were' (third‑person plural).
segments.s693-g56: به گیتی درون is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی + درون intensifies the sense of location 'within the world'); کامگاران is the predicate plural 'the successful/fulfilled ones'; بدند again is the past copula 'they were.' Word order is topical: location fronted, then predicate and copula.
segments.s693-g65: ازین پس (از + این + پس) is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from now on' or 'after this' (archaic spelling 'ازین'). The clause uses the correlative negation نه ... نه ... before the verb جویم: نه آرام جویم نه خواب = 'I will seek neither rest nor sleep.' جویم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/optative from جستن (here functioning as a simple present/future: 'I seek/will seek').
segments.s693-g66: مگر introduces an exception/conditional 'unless' or 'except that.' برکشم is here to be read as a 1st-person singular verb 'I pull/lift' (archaic/Bayṣelectional form built on بر- + کشیدن); the supplied lemma senses did not include this verbal meaning so sense is marked -1. دامن is the object 'hem/skirt,' and از تیره آب is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the dark water' (از = 'from'; تیره = 'dark'). Together: 'unless I pull my hem out of the dark water.'
segments.s693-g67: چو here is conditional 'if' (classical/poetic use). رومی is the object 'a Roman' (i.e. someone from Rûm/Byzantium). نیابیم is ن + یابیم (1st-person-plural subjunctive/present negative form of یافتن/یابیدن) and functions as 'we do not find/cannot find' — note the verb form is subjunctive/optative typical in conditional clauses. فریادرس is a predicate noun 'rescuer/succor' (the thing/person sought).
segments.s693-g68: به نزدیک خاقان is a prepositional phrase 'to the (near) presence of the Khaghan' (به = 'to/toward', نزدیک = 'near' used idiomatically to mean 'in the presence of'). فرستیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative 'we shall/send' (we will send or let us send). کس is an indefinite 'someone' (we will send someone). Word order places the destination phrase before the verb, common in Persian.
segments.s693-g69: سخن هرچ گفتم literally 'the word(s) whatever I said' — سخن 'speech/words', هرچ 'whatever'. گفتم is the past 1st-person singular 'I said' (the supplied lemma senses list nouns, so the verbal sense is marked -1). همه خیره شد = 'everyone was astonished' where خیره 'astonished/staring' + شد 'became' (past). Word order places the object phrase before the verb, then states the reaction.
segments.s693-g70: که introduces an explanatory/result clause 'for/that.' آب روان is the subject 'the flowing/running water' (آب = water; روان = 'flowing' here). از + بنه indicates origin/source ('from the بنه'); the listed senses for بنه do not clearly match the poetic sense, so its precise lexical sense is marked -1. تیره شد is a compound predicate 'became dark' (تیره = dark, شد = 'became').
segments.s693-g71: فرستادگانم is the subject 'my messengers/envoys.' چوآیند is a fused conditional/temporal 'چو + آیند' meaning 'when/if they come' (here 'when they return'); باز is an adverb 'back/again.' Together: 'when my messengers come back.'
segments.s693-g72: بدین is etymologically به + این 'in/at this' (the tokenization provided splits it into ب + دین; دین's listed senses are unrelated, so that item is marked -1). شارستان 'town/city' plus در 'in' give 'in this town.' نمانم is the verb نمانَم (ن- negation + مانم) 'I will not remain/stay' (first-person singular present/subjunctive used to express future/intent), and دراز 'long' modifies the duration: 'I will not remain long.' Word order places the locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s693-g41: چنین (an adverb) modifies the verb and means 'thus/so'; داد is the simple past 3rd-person singular of دادن 'to give' and here governs the noun پاسخ (object) — the verb precedes its object as is common in poetic Persian ("gave an answer"). که‌گر is a fused conditional particle (که + گر) meaning 'if' that introduces the following subordinate clause. زین is the bound form of ز این ('from this') used poetically; سخن is the noun 'speech/words' as the object of زین.
segments.s693-g42: که here functions as the complementizer 'that/which' linking the conditional clause; پیش آمد is a light-verb collocation meaning 'happened' or 'came before' (پیش 'ago/before' + آمد 'happen/come'); از marks source 'from'; روزگار کهن is a genitive phrase 'the ancient times' with کهن modifying روزگار.
segments.s693-g43: همی is an adverb of continuative aspect meaning 'ever/still' placed before the prepositional phrase. بر دل 'upon the heart' is the prepositional object; این یاد 'this remembrance' is the direct object/phrase being taken to heart. باید is the impersonal modal 'must/ought', and گرفت is the past-stem used after باید to form the necessity ('must take/keep'); together باید گرفت = 'one must take' (impersonal construction).
segments.s693-g44: This line repeats the obligation in a wishful/optative form: همه رنجها 'all the sorrows' is the topic; باد is the classical optative particle meaning 'may' or 'let it be that' and precedes the modal phrase باید گرفت, so the sequence باد باید گرفت expresses a hortatory/wishful necessity ('may it be that ... must be taken' → idiomatically 'let them be borne' or 'may they be endured').
segments.s693-g45: گرفتیم is the simple past 1st-person plural 'we took/seized' (or 'we took our leave' depending on context); و is the coordinating 'and'; گشتیم is the past 1st-person plural of گشتن meaning 'we turned/returned' here; زین (poetic contraction of ز این) means 'from this'; مرز 'frontier/border' is the object of the preposition; باز is an adverb 'back/again' indicating motion back from the frontier.
segments.s693-g46: شما را places 'you' in the role of affected party (را is the definite object/contrast marker); مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest' used to express a wish that something not happen; به introduces the domain 'to/with respect to' and ایران is the place/realm; نیاز here means 'need' or 'lack' — the whole clause is a conventional benediction: 'may it not be that you need Iran.'
segments.s693-g47: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (a verbal/nominative stem meaning 'look/see') plus the imperative کن 'do' — together 'look!' or 'behold!'. کنون is the adverb 'now/presently' modifying the imperative. نا is the short negative/exclamatory particle (classical usage) placed before the following noun phrase; in poetry it can function as an emphatic or archaising particle before nouns (here immediately before نیاکان ما).
segments.s693-g48: گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/selected' modifying the following nouns; جهاندار is a grandiose title 'world-ruler/sovereign' used here as a substantive; و is 'and'; پاکان is the plural 'the pure (ones)' and ما 'our' is a possessive pronoun attached after the noun (classical placement: noun + pronoun). The phrase lists illustrious qualities/people associated with the speaker's ancestors.
segments.s693-g33: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the noun phrase. گفتار is the head noun 'speech/utterance' and is followed by the modifiers بیدار دانندگان: بیدار ('awake/alert') modifies دانندگان ('the learned/knowers'); the genitive/linking relation is implicit (poetic omission of ezāfe), so the phrase means 'the speech of the wakeful/alert knowers.'
segments.s693-g34: سخنهای is the plural noun 'utterances/words' (note the plural/indefinite -های). دیرینه is an adjective 'ancient/long‑standing' following the noun, and خوانندگان is the plural agent 'reciters/chanters'; again the linking (ِezāfe) is not written in the verse, so the phrase reads as 'the ancient utterances of the reciters.' The whole segment is a nominal phrase parallel to the previous segment.
segments.s693-g35: چو here is the temporal 'when'. The verb آمد ('came') appears before the subject (suar/سوار), a common poetic inversion: 'when came near Khosrow the rider'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near', placing the action spatially 'near Khosrow.'
segments.s693-g36: بگفت is an archaic/classical past form with the prefix بـ (equivalent to گفت 'said') and functions as the main verb 'he said/told'. آنچ is a fused demonstrative 'that which' introducing reported content, and بشنید is past 'he heard' (the content he had heard). با نامدار is a prepositional phrase 'with/to the renowned one' (با here functions dative/recipient in classical usage), so the clause means 'he told the renowned one that which he had heard.'
segments.s693-g37: همان is the emphatic determiner 'that very/the same'. نامه قیصر is a genitive—'Caesar's letter'. او را combines the pronoun او (he/him) with the object marker را placed before the verb; سپرد is the past 'entrusted/handed over', so the clause reads 'he handed him that same letter of Caesar.' The object marker را precedes the verb as is normal in Persian.
segments.s693-g38: سخنهای قیصر is 'the words/utterances of Caesar' (genitive). The item برو in this context functions as a classical contraction/particle meaning 'to him' (from به + او) or otherwise marks direction toward the recipient; this particular sense is not listed in the supplied senses, so it is marked -1. برشمرد is the past 'recounted/enumerated', completing 'he recounted Caesar's words to him.'
segments.s693-g39: چو is the temporal 'when'. خسرو is the subject and follows the verb بدید ('saw')—another poetic inversion. آن is the demonstrative 'that/it' (pointing to whatever was shown), and دلش تنگ شد is an idiomatic construction: دل تنگ شدن 'to have the heart become tight' = to be distressed/longing/sad; دل + possessive ش ('his') + تنگ شد ('became tight').
segments.s693-g40: رخانش here refers to his countenance/complexion (the noun form is poetic); the supplied lemma senses do not match this precise meaning, so its sense is marked -1. ز اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of thought/worry', and بی‌رنگ شد literally 'became colorless'—i.e. 'his face grew pale.' The structure is cause (ز اندیشه) → result (بی‌رنگ شد).

sentences.s693-s41: A rider of Khosrow came up close and so reached the renowned Caesar's gate.
sentences.s693-s1: Because of the stranger, the Caesar took his place to attend to the matter; full of thought he sat down with his adviser.
sentences.s693-s2: He said thus to the mobad: 'O seeker of justice, the world has taken away our refuge.'
sentences.s693-s3: Let us make (him) so that he may become strong by force; and thereby the lesser one will be left ill and bereft of the gazelle.
sentences.s693-s4: Then the adviser said thus to the Caesar: that (from) the philosophers of clean counsel...
sentences.s693-s29: They would not tolerate anyone's arrogance, fierceness, or ignorance.
sentences.s693-s30: Now these words have no value, for they count for nothing in a dragon's breath.
sentences.s693-s31: Let someone go to Caesar to deliver my greeting, and tell him that the speech is without warp and weft.
sentences.s693-s32: Great men do not place themselves before wisdom; in the end, both good and bad pass.
sentences.s693-s13: Even at that time a cry will go forth from Rome and (that cry) will raise up all the frontiers and the land.
sentences.s693-s14: Keep (it) as a fitting, tearful remembrance with wisdom; may the sayings of the Iranians endure.
sentences.s693-s15: When the Caesar heard of them, he conceived another thought then.
sentences.s693-s16: He sent a horseman to the king's presence; someone wrote a letter and pointed out the way.
sentences.s693-s9: Because of so much plunder and warfare and hanging, and from that same innocent people standing stunned, blood was spilled.
sentences.s693-s10: Now the pure God, because of bad deeds, has brought upon them an evil outcome.
sentences.s693-s11: Choose one silence from among them, when the fortune of the Sasanians becomes frankincense.
sentences.s693-s12: If Khosrow obtains that royal hat, he will lift his head like the moon.
sentences.s693-s5: We must have a few alert‑hearted men who will bind their hearts to this task with us.
sentences.s693-s6: The famed Caesar sent someone; from those philosophers four set out.
sentences.s693-s7: Young and old of Roman stock recalled ancient words.
sentences.s693-s8: That we, until Alexander came into this world, were of Iranian stock, secretly weary.
sentences.s693-s37: He said to the Iranians, "Issue orders; do not let these words break your hearts."
sentences.s693-s38: For God the victorious is our ally; chivalry and the people's cause are our concern.
sentences.s693-s39: He took those words to his heart; the wretch sent the letter into (or into the) hands.
sentences.s693-s40: In this manner he wrote a proclamation, of every kind — both fair and foul.
sentences.s693-s25: They waged war on oppression; if there was ill‑treatment, look to the elders who remember it.
sentences.s693-s26: If one deems it fitting, let him ask the wise man of Rome whether this evil has come from the crow or from the land.
sentences.s693-s27: For whoever becomes victorious in battle, by the Creator he becomes self‑sufficient.
sentences.s693-s28: Our ancestors were renowned; in the world they were prosperous/successful.
sentences.s693-s33: From now on I will seek neither rest nor sleep, unless I pull my hem out of the dark water.
sentences.s693-s34: If we do not find a Roman to come to our aid, we will send someone to the Khaghan's presence.
sentences.s693-s35: Whatever I said, everyone was astonished; for the running water grew dark at its source.
sentences.s693-s36: When my messengers return, I will not remain long in this town.
sentences.s693-s21: He gave this reply: "If, from this speech that came in ancient times..."
sentences.s693-s22: One should always take this remembrance to heart; may all sorrows be borne.
sentences.s693-s23: We took (them) and turned back from this frontier; may you never be in need of Iran.
sentences.s693-s24: Look now: our forefathers — our chosen sovereigns and our pure ones.
sentences.s693-s17: From the clear speech of the wakeful, and the ancient utterances of the reciters.
sentences.s693-s18: When the horseman came near Khosrow, he told the renowned one what he had heard.
sentences.s693-s19: He handed him that very letter of Caesar; he recounted to him the words of Caesar.
sentences.s693-s20: When Khosrow saw it, his heart grew tight (he was saddened); his face went pale from anxiety.
